description: JWT claim -> Identity coercion parity (Algorithm A-AUTH). Each case supplies a JWT payload; the runner signs it with `secret` (HS256), calls authenticate(), and asserts the derived Identity (or a None/reject). Coercion is the Rust-strict canonical rule documented in docs/features/auth.md.

test_cases.description: A scalar numeric sub is coerced (PyJWT verify_sub disabled so it reaches coercion).
An explicit empty string type is preserved, NOT defaulted to 'user'.
Object/array role elements are dropped; scalar elements are coerced.

reject_cases.description: A non-scalar sub is not a valid identity -> token rejected (authenticate returns None).
exp in the past -> None (no exception).
Token signed with a different secret than the authenticator -> None.

known_divergences: Python additionally sets PyJWT options['verify_sub']=False so a scalar numeric sub reaches the shared coercion helper instead of PyJWT's RFC string-only rejection. TypeScript/Rust have no such decode-time check. The observable result asserted here is identical across all three.
The shared helper is named _claim_to_string (Python) / claimToString (TS) / claim_to_string (Rust). Name is not asserted; only the coerced Identity is.
